tweets.content:
Daily US-Israel-Iran War Brief:
27th Jun 2026:

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief

● CENTCOM conducted strikes on Iranian missile and drone storage sites as well as coastal radar installations, in direct response to Iran’s drone attack on the vessel M/V Ever Lovely.

● Iran’s Foreign Minister condemned the US strikes as a violation of the US-Iran MoU and Article 2(4) of the UN Charter.

● The French-owned container ship CMA CGM GALAPAGOS successfully transited the Strait of Hormuz using an Iran-designated safe route.

● The Panama-flagged VLCC KIKU, laden with Qatar Energy oil, was struck during its outbound transit of the Strait of Hormuz.

● Bahrain was targeted by drones this morning. Bahraini air defences intercepted several projectiles.

END OF THE BRIEF

- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief

● Iraq’s Ministry of Oil denied speculation that the country threatened to leave OPEC, stating such claims do not reflect the official government position.

● The Singapore-flagged container ship EVER LOVELY was struck by an unknown projectile near its bridge while departing the Strait of Hormuz area. The US blamed Iran for the attack. All 21 crew members were reported safe.

tweets.title: Certain pro-import circles seem to have found a new phrase today: “the click jibe.” What they need to understand is that, by nature, many Indians dislike paying billions of dollars for imported weapon systems only to later read headlines saying those systems have been “cleared for export to India.” When you’ve paid in full, such language feels demeaning to people with self-respect. Also, when things get stuck after paying in full it hurts too.. be it system or LRU (engine). That is why criticism of excessive imports, and support for indigenous products even if imperfect in their early stages resonates with a much larger audience than many assume. So next time when you come up with that “click” jibe do some backup work 😅
tweets.content:
Certain pro-import circles seem to have found a new phrase today: “the click jibe.”

What they need to understand is that, by nature, many Indians dislike paying billions of dollars for imported weapon systems only to later read headlines saying those systems have been “cleared for export to India.” When you’ve paid in full, such language feels demeaning to people with self-respect. Also, when things get stuck after paying in full it hurts too.. be it system or LRU (engine).

That is why criticism of excessive imports, and support for indigenous products even if imperfect in their early stages resonates with a much larger audience than many assume.

So next time when you come up with that “click” jibe do some backup work 😅

tweets.title: The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has successfully conducted a hot test of its semi-cryogenic engine power head, achieving 175 tonnes of thrust, around 88% of its target. tweets.title: There are two types of airforces : Rich and Poor News : It’s official: F-35s are now being delivered without radars due to delays in the AN/APG-85 AESA radar program. It highlights two broad approaches to defence procurement. ✅ One approach is followed by countries that buy finished products. They expect complete, ready-to-fight systems because their military’s job is to fight wars, not participate in weapon development. ✅ The other approach is followed by countries that treat suppliers as long-term partners. They understand that complex defence programs will face engineering and supply-chain disruptions, especially during periods of heightened geopolitical tension. These airforces are “poor” and do not see importing an alternative at will is often an option, they work through the delays together rather than expecting perfection from day one.
tweets.content:
There are two types of airforces : Rich and Poor

News : It’s official: F-35s are now being delivered without radars due to delays in the AN/APG-85 AESA radar program.

It highlights two broad approaches to defence procurement.

✅ One approach is followed by countries that buy finished products. They expect complete, ready-to-fight systems because their military’s job is to fight wars, not participate in weapon development.

✅ The other approach is followed by countries that treat suppliers as long-term partners. They understand that complex defence programs will face engineering and supply-chain disruptions, especially during periods of heightened geopolitical tension.

These airforces are “poor” and do not see importing an alternative at will is often an option, they work through the delays together rather than expecting perfection from day one.

Kishtwar Incident: Army's Version of Events (Summary)

According to the Army's account, the incident began after Rashtriya Rifles (RR) civilian vehicles, engaged in official duties, inadvertently delayed the Deputy Commissioner's convoy by 10–20 minutes. Following the delay, the civil administration allegedly ordered the immediate seizure of the Army vehicles, which were taken to Atholi Police Station.

The Army claims the seizure was carried out without following the prescribed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs). In an effort to resolve the matter peacefully, the Commanding Officer reportedly sent a Company Commander and a Quick Reaction Team (QRT) to the police station to seek the release of the vehicles through dialogue.

The Army alleges that police personnel locked the station gates and declined repeated requests for discussion. It further claims that after attempts at communication failed, the gates were breached. During the confrontation, a police official allegedly cocked his weapon and threatened the Army personnel, prompting the QRT to intervene, disarm the individual, and secure the area. A physical altercation followed, involving police personnel, including the Station House Officer (SHO).

The Army maintains that the subsequent FIR filed against its personnel resulted from a breakdown in coordination between the civil administration and the Army. The matter remains under investigation, and the claims made by both sides have not been judicially established.

tweets.title: Kishtwar Incident: Army's Version of Events (Summary) According to the Army's account, the incident began after Rashtriya Rifles (RR) civilian vehicles, engaged in official duties, inadvertently delayed the Deputy Commissioner's convoy by 10–20 minutes. Following the delay, the civil administration allegedly ordered the immediate seizure of the Army vehicles, which were taken to Atholi Police Station. The Army claims the seizure was carried out without following the prescribed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs). In an effort to resolve the matter peacefully, the Commanding Officer reportedly sent a Company Commander and a Quick Reaction Team (QRT) to the police station to seek the release of the vehicles through dialogue. The Army alleges that police personnel locked the station gates and declined repeated requests for discussion. It further claims that after attempts at communication failed, the gates were breached. During the confrontation, a police official allegedly cocked his weapon and threatened the Army personnel, prompting the QRT to intervene, disarm the individual, and secure the area. A physical altercation followed, involving police personnel, including the Station House Officer (SHO). The Army maintains that the subsequent FIR filed against its personnel resulted from a breakdown in coordination between the civil administration and the Army. The matter remains under investigation, and the claims made by both sides have not been judicially established.

BREAKING: J&K Police Files FIR Against Rashtriya Rifles Personnel in Kishtwar Incident

Jammu & Kashmir Police have registered an FIR against the Commanding Officer of 17 Rashtriya Rifles, a Major, a Naib Subedar, and 30–40 Army personnel over an alleged incident at Atholi Police Station, Kishtwar, on June 24.

The FIR includes charges under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), including attempt to murder, and the Prevention of Damage to Public Property Act. Police allege Army personnel entered the police station, assaulted officers, and damaged government property.

The Indian Army has acknowledged the incident, said it is examining the matter through established institutional mechanisms, and stated it will fully cooperate with the investigation. The allegations are under investigation, and no judicial findings have been made.
